curated_content.name: MDN Web Docs - HTML Best Practices

alt_text_required.statement: The alt attribute is required on all elements for accessibility and SEO; use descriptive text for meaningful images, empty alt="" for decorative images (never omit the attribute)
alt_text_required.applies_to: All img elements in HTML documents

labels_required.statement: All form inputs must have associated <label> elements for accessibility; clicking label focuses input and screen readers announce the label text
labels_required.applies_to: All form input, select, and textarea elements

use_semantic_elements.statement: Semantic HTML elements (<header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, <footer>) should be used instead of generic <div> elements when appropriate for accessibility and SEO
use_semantic_elements.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

prefer_semantic_elements.title: Prefer Semantic HTML Elements Over Generic Containers
prefer_semantic_elements.description: Choose semantic HTML5 elements (<header>, <nav>, <main>, <article>, <section>, <aside>, <footer>) instead of generic <div> elements when the content has semantic meaning
prefer_semantic_elements.rationale: Semantic HTML provides meaning to content structure, improving accessibility (screen readers understand document structure), SEO (search engines understand content hierarchy), maintainability (code is self-documenting), and follows HTML Living Standard guidance. Generic containers should only be used for styling/layout when no semantic element fits.
prefer_semantic_elements.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

when_not_applicable: Pure styling/layout wrappers with no semantic meaning

options.name: Semantic HTML Elements
options.reason: HTML Living Standard recommended approach: provides meaning, accessibility, SEO benefits

pros: Accessibility: Screen readers understand document structure and can navigate by landmarks
SEO: Search engines understand content hierarchy and importance
Maintainability: Code is self-documenting, intent is clear
Standards compliance: Follows HTML Living Standard best practices
Future-proof: Semantic meaning persists across updates
No additional cost: Same as using <div>, just different element name
cons: Learning curve: Developers must understand which semantic element to use
Older browsers: Very old browsers (IE8-) may need polyfills (rare concern in 2025)
Flexibility: Cannot use same element for multiple semantic purposes

context_requirements: Content has semantic meaning (not just styling container)
Modern browser target (essentially all browsers in 2025)
Want accessibility and SEO benefits

options.name: Generic Containers (div, span)
options.reason: Only appropriate when content has NO semantic meaning; acceptable for styling/layout wrappers

pros: Flexibility: Can be used for any purpose without semantic constraints
Simplicity: No need to choose correct semantic element
Universal browser support: Works everywhere including very old browsers
cons: No accessibility benefit: Screen readers cannot identify content purpose
No SEO benefit: Search engines cannot understand content hierarchy
Not self-documenting: Code intent unclear without additional class names
Violates best practices: Does not follow HTML Living Standard guidance
Missed opportunities: Loses semantic meaning that could benefit users

context_requirements: Content has NO semantic meaning (pure styling/layout wrapper)
No appropriate semantic element exists
Container for JavaScript manipulation or CSS styling only

decision_factors: Content meaning: Has semantic meaning? → Semantic element (yes) or <div> (no)
Content type: Article/blog post? → <article>; Section of content? → <section>
Styling only: Pure layout/styling wrapper? → <div> acceptable
Best practice: Follow HTML Living Standard? → Semantic elements (yes, always recommended)

use_html5_validation.title: Use HTML5 Validation Attributes as Base Layer
use_html5_validation.description: Implement HTML5 validation attributes (required, pattern, min/max, minlength/maxlength) as the base validation layer, optionally enhanced with JavaScript for complex validation
use_html5_validation.rationale: HTML5 validation provides immediate validation feedback, works without JavaScript, is accessible by default, and follows progressive enhancement principles. JavaScript validation should enhance, not replace, HTML5 validation. This approach ensures validation works even if JavaScript fails to load.
use_html5_validation.source: WHATWG HTML Living Standard, MDN Web Docs

when_applicable: Need basic validation (required, format, length, range)
when_not_applicable: Complex validation requiring external data (e.g., username availability check)
Need custom error message positioning/styling (can still use HTML5 + JavaScript enhancement)

options.name: HTML5 Validation Attributes (Base Layer)
options.reason: Standards-based, accessible, works without JavaScript, progressive enhancement

pros: Works without JavaScript: Validation even if JS fails to load
Progressive enhancement: Base functionality always available
Accessible: Built-in browser validation messages are accessible
No code required: Just attributes, no JavaScript
Mobile friendly: Appropriate keyboards (email, number, tel)
Standards-compliant: Follows HTML Living Standard
Browser support: Universal support in modern browsers
cons: Limited customization: Error message styling varies by browser
No cross-field validation: Cannot validate field relationships
No async validation: Cannot check against server (e.g., unique username)
Pattern complexity: Regular expressions can be complex
Browser differences: Slight UI differences across browsers

context_requirements: Basic validation needs (required, format, length, range)
Want validation even without JavaScript
Acceptable to have browser default error messages
Modern browser target

options.name: JavaScript Validation (Enhancement Layer)
options.reason: Enhances HTML5 validation for complex scenarios, custom UI, async validation

pros: Custom error messages: Full control over error text and positioning
Custom UI: Style error messages to match design
Cross-field validation: Validate relationships (password match, date ranges)
Async validation: Check against server (username availability, email uniqueness)
Complex logic: Business rules beyond simple format validation
Real-time validation: Validate as user types
cons: Requires JavaScript: If JS fails, only HTML5 validation remains
More code: Must write validation logic and error handling
Accessibility considerations: Must ensure custom errors are accessible
Maintenance: More code to maintain and test
Performance: Real-time validation can impact performance if complex

context_requirements: Complex validation needs (cross-field, async, business rules)
Need custom error message UI to match design
JavaScript is available and working
Have development resources for implementation

options.name: No Validation (Server-Only)
options.reason: Poor user experience; not recommended except for very simple forms

pros: Simple: No client-side validation code
Server validation required anyway: Validation in one place
cons: Poor UX: User must submit form to see errors (full page reload)
Increased server load: Invalid submissions reach server
Accessibility issues: No immediate feedback
Violates best practices: HTML5 validation is free and easy
Not progressive enhancement: Worse experience for all users

context_requirements: Extremely simple form with one or two fields
Very low traffic (server load not a concern)
Acceptable to have poor user experience

decision_factors: Validation complexity: Simple (format, length, required)? → HTML5 only; Complex (cross-field, async)? → HTML5 + JavaScript
Custom UI needed: Browser errors OK? → HTML5 only; Need custom styling? → HTML5 + JavaScript
Progressive enhancement: Want validation without JS? → HTML5 base + JS enhancement
Development resources: Limited? → HTML5 only; Available? → HTML5 + JavaScript enhancement
Best practice: Follow standards? → HTML5 validation always, optionally enhanced with JavaScript

use_responsive_images.title: Use Responsive Images (srcset/sizes) for Performance
use_responsive_images.description: Implement responsive images using srcset and sizes attributes to serve appropriately sized images based on device, improving performance and reducing bandwidth
use_responsive_images.rationale: Responsive images reduce bandwidth (mobile users don't download desktop-sized images), improve performance (faster page loads), enhance user experience, and are supported by all modern browsers. The browser automatically selects the best image based on device characteristics.
use_responsive_images.source: MDN Web Docs, WHATWG HTML Living Standard

when_applicable: Images displayed at different sizes on different devices
Mobile users are significant portion of traffic
when_not_applicable: Small images (<50KB) where multiple sizes aren't worth the effort
Images always displayed at exact same size across all devices
Icons or logos that are already small

options.name: Responsive Images (srcset/sizes)
options.reason: Modern standard for performance-optimized images, automatic browser selection

pros: Performance: Mobile devices download smaller images (faster load, less bandwidth)
Automatic: Browser selects best image based on device capabilities
User experience: Faster page loads improve satisfaction
SEO: Page speed is ranking factor for search engines
Cost savings: Reduced bandwidth usage for users on limited data plans
Universal support: All modern browsers support srcset/sizes
cons: Setup effort: Must generate multiple image sizes
Storage: Multiple versions of each image stored
Build process: May require image processing in build pipeline
Complexity: srcset/sizes syntax more complex than simple src
Not automatic: Requires planning which sizes to generate

context_requirements: Images displayed at different sizes on different viewports
Performance optimization is priority
Can generate multiple image sizes (manually or automated)
Modern browser target

options.name: Single Image (src only)
options.reason: Simple but suboptimal for performance; acceptable for small images or when effort outweighs benefit

pros: Simple: Only one image file to manage
No build process: No need to generate multiple sizes
Less storage: Only one image file stored
Easier to implement: Just src attribute
cons: Performance cost: Mobile users download oversized images
Bandwidth waste: Downloading pixels that won't be displayed
Slower page loads: Larger file sizes take longer to download
Poor mobile experience: Long load times on slow connections
SEO impact: Page speed affects search rankings
User experience: Frustrating for users on limited data plans

context_requirements: Small images (<50KB) where optimization won't provide significant benefit
Images always displayed at same size across all devices
Development time very limited
Performance is not a concern (internal tools, high-bandwidth users)

decision_factors: Image size: Large images (>200KB)? → Responsive images (yes) or Single (no if <50KB)
Device variation: Different sizes on mobile/desktop? → Responsive images (yes) or Single (no if same size)
Performance priority: Important? → Responsive images (yes) or Single (no if not critical)
Mobile traffic: Significant mobile users? → Responsive images (yes) or Single (no if desktop-only)
Development resources: Can generate multiple sizes? → Responsive images (yes) or Single (no if limited)
Best practice: Follow performance recommendations? → Responsive images (yes, for non-trivial images)
